agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA display from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a significant change to a core customer-facing promise in a logistics/courier system. ETA precision directly affects customer trust, operational accountability, and competitive positioning.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Order Fulfillment — the bounded context responsible for communicating delivery commitments to customers and downstream systems.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — the delivery commitment communicated to the customer, which sets expectations and triggers downstream behaviors (e.g., recipient availability, re-delivery)."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"The time range within which a delivery is promised. Can be an exact timestamp, a time slot (morning/afternoon), or a date-only commitment."},{"term":"SLA","definition":"Service Level Agreement — the contractual or implied promise about delivery speed, often tied to service tier (standard, express, same-day)."},{"term":"Service Tier","definition":"The level of delivery service selected (e.g., standard, express, same-day), each carrying different ETA precision expectations."},{"term":"Last Mile","definition":"The final leg of delivery from distribution hub to recipient, where ETA variability is highest."},{"term":"Delivery Promise","definition":"The commitment shown to the customer at checkout and in tracking — this IS the contract from the customer's perspective."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"The delivery promise shown to the customer must accurately reflect the service tier purchased. Express and same-day tiers inherently require sub-day time precision.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A same-day delivery displayed as 'by Friday' is meaningless — the entire value proposition of premium tiers is time precision. Showing only a date for a same-day delivery breaks the fundamental contract.","testable_assertion":"IF service_tier IN (same_day, express) THEN ETA display MUST include sub-day precision (time window or exact time), not date-only."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"ETA display format must not reduce precision below what was promised at checkout. If the customer was shown a time at purchase, tracking must not downgrade to date-only.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Reducing precision post-purchase violates the delivery promise that influenced the buying decision. This is a contract integrity issue.","testable_assertion":"IF checkout_eta_precision = timestamp THEN tracking_eta_precision >= timestamp"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Date-only ETAs ('by Friday') must define a clear cutoff time for SLA measurement and customer expectation (e.g., end of business day, 23:59).","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The specific cutoff time is a policy decision, but SOME cutoff must exist for SLA compliance tracking.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a date-only ETA of 'by Friday' WHEN Friday ends THEN the system must evaluate SLA compliance against a defined cutoff time"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Any change to ETA display format must preserve the ability to calculate and report on-time delivery rates with equivalent or better accuracy.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Operational reporting is critical but the specific metrics are policy-configurable.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN the new date-only format WHEN on-time delivery rate is calculated THEN the metric must use a well-defined cutoff, not become unmeasurable"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"B2B customers with contractual SLAs specifying time-of-day delivery commitments must continue receiving time-precise ETAs regardless of any display change for B2C.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Contractual SLAs are legal obligations. Unilaterally reducing ETA precision would breach contracts.","testable_assertion":"IF customer.type = B2B AND contract.sla_includes_time_precision = true THEN ETA display MUST include time-of-day precision"}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer purchases standard delivery","when":"They view the delivery ETA in tracking","then":"The ETA is displayed as a date-only window (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday') with no time-of-day component"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A customer purchases same-day or express delivery","when":"They view the delivery ETA in tracking","then":"The ETA retains sub-day precision (time window or exact time), NOT date-only"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A customer was shown an exact timestamp ETA at checkout","when":"They later view tracking for the same order","then":"The tracking ETA precision is equal to or better than what was shown at checkout"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A date-only ETA of 'Delivery by Friday' is shown","when":"The delivery arrives at 23:50 on Friday","then":"The system records this as an on-time delivery based on the defined cutoff policy"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A B2B customer with a time-precise SLA contract","when":"They view delivery ETA","then":"The ETA includes time-of-day precision per their contract terms"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"An order transitions from 'out for delivery' to 'delivered'","when":"The actual delivery time is recorded","then":"SLA compliance is evaluated against the original ETA commitment, using the defined date-only cutoff time where applicable"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"The ETA format change is deployed","when":"Historical delivery performance reports are generated","then":"Reports clearly distinguish between orders under the old (timestamp) and new (date-only) ETA regimes to prevent misleading metric comparisons"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Same-day delivery displayed as date-only","expected_behavior":"MUST NOT happen. Same-day deliveries require time precision — showing 'by today' with no time is useless to the recipient trying to be available."},{"scenario":"Cross-midnight delivery (package in transit at 23:59 on the ETA date)","expected_behavior":"Clear policy needed: does 'by Friday' mean by 23:59 Friday, by end of business Friday, or by the start of the next business day? This directly impacts SLA compliance rates."},{"scenario":"Timezone differences between sender, recipient, and warehouse","expected_behavior":"Date-only ETAs must be evaluated in the RECIPIENT's local timezone, not the warehouse or system timezone. 'By Friday' means Friday in the recipient's timezone."},{"scenario":"Customer contacts support asking 'when exactly will it arrive?'","expected_behavior":"Support agents need access to more granular internal ETAs even if the customer-facing display is date-only. Removing time precision from internal systems would cripple support."},{"scenario":"Re-delivery after failed first attempt","expected_behavior":"The new ETA for re-delivery must follow the same display rules. If the original was date-only, the re-delivery ETA should also be date-only but for the new date."},{"scenario":"ETA date passes without delivery","expected_behavior":"The system must detect the SLA breach and trigger appropriate workflows (customer notification, refund eligibility, escalation) based on the date-only cutoff time."},{"scenario":"Integration partners consuming ETA via API","expected_behavior":"API contracts may expose exact timestamps. Changing the display format must not break API response schemas or reduce precision for downstream integrators who depend on time-of-day data."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"MODERATE-TO-HIGH NEGATIVE RISK. Reduced ETA precision for premium tiers would erode the value proposition that justifies higher pricing. If express/same-day customers see only date-level ETAs, conversion rates for premium tiers will likely drop. Conversely, for standard delivery, vaguer ETAs may reduce refund/compensation claims triggered by narrow misses (e.g., arriving at 14:45 when ETA was 14:30). Net financial impact depends entirely on whether premium tiers are excluded from this change.","operational":"MIXED. Positive: Wider delivery windows reduce pressure on last-mile operations and may improve on-time delivery rates by definition (easier to hit a date than an exact time). Negative: Less precise commitments reduce operational accountability and may mask inefficiencies. Internal routing and scheduling systems should retain full time precision regardless of display changes. Risk of 'what gets measured gets managed' — vaguer promises may lead to vaguer performance.","user":"HIGH NEGATIVE for transparency-sensitive customers. Exact ETAs allow recipients to plan their day (be home for delivery, arrange for someone to receive). Date-only ETAs force customers to be available all day or risk missed deliveries, increasing failed delivery attempts. Customer satisfaction surveys in logistics consistently show ETA precision as a top-3 factor. Competitors offering time-window delivery (Amazon, UPS My Choice) set high expectations. However, UNRELIABLE exact ETAs are worse than honest date-only windows — if current exact ETAs are frequently wrong, this change may actually improve trust.","risk":"1) Legal/contractual risk if B2B SLAs require time precision and this change is applied broadly. 2) Competitive risk as market trend is toward MORE precision, not less. 3) Metric integrity risk — historical on-time delivery rates become incomparable across the format change. 4) Integration risk if downstream systems or partners depend on time-precise ETAs via APIs.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End Customers (B2C)","impact":"Lose ability to plan around a specific delivery time. May increase missed deliveries and re-delivery costs. Impact is severe for time-sensitive deliveries (perishables, medical supplies, gifts)."},{"group":"B2B Customers with SLA Contracts","impact":"Potentially catastrophic if time-precise SLAs are breached. Must be explicitly excluded from any display format change."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Increased call volume from customers asking 'when exactly will it arrive?' Support agents need internal access to granular ETAs even if customer display is date-only."},{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Reduced external accountability may lead to schedule slippage over time. Internal systems must retain full time precision for routing optimization."},{"group":"Product / Marketing","impact":"Harder to market delivery speed as a differentiator. Competitors offering time-window delivery gain positioning advantage."},{"group":"Finance / Revenue","impact":"Risk of reduced premium tier conversion if express/same-day ETAs lose precision. Potential offset from reduced SLA violation refunds on standard tier."},{"group":"Integration Partners / API Consumers","impact":"Must verify that API contracts are not affected. Display format change should be presentation-layer only, not data-layer."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The proposal is clear in WHAT it wants to change but critically ambiguous in scope, motivation, and applicability. Without knowing which service tiers are affected, why the change is being proposed (unreliable exact ETAs? technical limitation? operational pressure?), and whether API/B2B contracts have been considered, I cannot confidently endorse or reject this change. The analysis above covers the domain implications comprehensively, but the right answer depends entirely on scoping decisions not specified in the proposal.","high_confidence_areas":["Premium/express tiers MUST retain sub-day ETA precision — this is a hard domain invariant","B2B contractual SLAs must be honored regardless of display changes","Internal systems must retain full time precision even if customer display changes","Timezone handling must use recipient's local timezone for date-only ETAs"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether this change applies to ALL tiers or only standard delivery — not specified","The business motivation driving this change (unreliable ETAs? cost reduction? technical debt?)","Whether API response schemas will also change or only UI display","What the defined cutoff time for 'by Friday' means operationally","Whether customer research or A/B testing has been done to validate user acceptance"],"assumptions":["This is a customer-facing display change, not a change to internal ETA calculation precision","The system has distinct service tiers with different delivery speed commitments","Some form of SLA compliance tracking exists and must continue to function","API consumers exist downstream who may depend on time-precise ETA data"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The proposal does not specify which service tiers are affected. Applying date-only ETAs to express or same-day delivery fundamentally breaks those service tiers' value proposition. Does this change apply to standard delivery only, or all tiers?","affected_stakeholders":["End Customers","Product/Marketing","Finance/Revenue"],"options":["A) Apply date-only ETAs to standard delivery only, retain time precision for express/same-day","B) Apply date-only ETAs to all tiers (strongly not recommended)","C) Introduce tiered display: date-only for standard, time-window for express, exact time for same-day"],"recommendation":"Option C — tiered ETA precision aligned to service tier. This is the industry standard pattern (UPS, FedEx, Amazon all do this)."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The business motivation for this change is not stated. If exact ETAs are frequently inaccurate, the correct fix may be improving ETA accuracy rather than hiding imprecision behind vaguer promises. The right solution depends on the root cause.","affected_stakeholders":["End Customers","Operations","Product"],"options":["A) Proceed with date-only if exact ETAs are proven unreliable and accuracy cannot be improved short-term","B) Invest in ETA accuracy improvement instead of reducing display precision","C) Hybrid: show date-only initially, narrow to time window as delivery approaches and confidence increases"],"recommendation":"Need the root cause data before deciding. If current exact ETAs have >30% miss rate, Option A or C is justified as a short-term measure. If miss rate is <15%, Option B is preferred."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Delivery by Friday' has no defined cutoff time. Does it mean by 17:00 (end of business), by 21:00 (last delivery run), or by 23:59? This directly affects SLA measurement, customer expectations, and operational planning.","affected_stakeholders":["End Customers","Operations","Customer Support","Finance"],"options":["A) End of business day (17:00 local)","B) End of delivery operations window (typically 20:00-21:00)","C) End of calendar day (23:59)"],"recommendation":"Option B — align with actual delivery operations window. Option C is technically easiest but sets unrealistic late-night delivery expectations. Option A is too restrictive and would hurt on-time rates."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA display from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a significant change to a core customer-facing promise in a logistics/courier system. ETA precision directly affects customer trust, operational accountability, and competitive positioning.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Order Fulfillment — the bounded context responsible

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this business domain change regarding delivery ETA display format.

This is a straightforward domain analysis request - no code to review, just a business impact analysis of changing from exact timestamp ETAs to date-only windows.

Let me classify: The proposal is clear but lacks critical context about WHY this change is being made, what the current system constraints are, and what customer segments are affected. However, the proposal itself is described clearly enough. The missing piece is the business justification and customer impact data. This is ambiguity - the spec is vague about the business rationale and implications.

Actually, let me reconsider. 

The proposal gives me the what but not the why - I don't know if this is driven by reliability issues with exact timestamps, customer complaints, or technical constraints. That's missing context rather than ambiguity. Still, I can work through the domain impact analysis even without the full business justification, though my confidence in the recommendations will be limited. The core change is clear enough: moving from precise timestamps to broader date windows in the logistics domain.
